questions.explanation: G1 is the first gap phase of interphase, when the cell grows and performs its normal functions before S-phase DNA replication. The image shows a cell with unreplicated chromatin and no mitotic spindle.

prompt.text: Sister chromatids condense.

questions.explanation: Sister chromatids first become visibly condensed during prophase, when chromatin compacts into discrete chromosomes. Condensation allows orderly attachment to the spindle and separation later in division.

prompt.text: Nucleoli disappear.

questions.explanation: Nucleoli disappear in prophase as ribosomal RNA synthesis pauses and the nucleolus disassembles. This accompanies chromosome condensation and nuclear reorganization at the start of mitosis.

prompt.text: Mitotic spindle begins to form.

questions.explanation: The mitotic spindle begins forming in prophase as centrosomes separate and microtubules polymerize. Spindle assembly is essential for later chromosome alignment and movement.

prompt.text: Centrosomes begin moving away from each other.

questions.explanation: Centrosomes migrate toward opposite poles during prophase, establishing the bipolar spindle. Their separation defines the axis along which chromosomes will be divided.
Prophase is marked by condensed chromosomes, spindle initiation, and centrosome separation—events visible in the phase diagram. Prometaphase follows once the nuclear envelope breaks down.

prompt.text: Nuclear envelope fragments.

questions.explanation: The nuclear envelope fragments in prometaphase, allowing spindle microtubules to reach chromosomes. This transition separates prophase from metaphase.

prompt.text: Microtubules from centrosomes begin invading the nuclear area.

questions.explanation: Prometaphase is when centrosomal microtubules invade the former nuclear area after envelope breakdown. Spindle fibers can then attach to kinetochores on condensing chromosomes.

prompt.text: Kinetochores have now formed at the centromere of each chromatid (2 per chromosome).

questions.explanation: Kinetochores assemble at centromeres during prometaphase as the nuclear envelope breaks down. Each sister chromatid has its own kinetochore, giving two per replicated chromosome.

prompt.text: Some microtubules begin to connect to the kinetochores becoming "kinetochore microtubules" which can move the chromosome.

questions.explanation: Kinetochore microtubules first connect to chromosomes in prometaphase, generating the pulling forces that congress chromosomes to the metaphase plate.

prompt.text: Non-kinetochore microtubules extending from one centrosome begin interacting with non-kinetochore microtubules originating from the other centrosome, causing the cell to lengthen.

questions.explanation: Non-kinetochore microtubules from opposite poles overlap and push apart during prometaphase, elongating the cell. This polar microtubule interaction helps position centrosomes at opposite ends.
Prometaphase features a fragmenting nuclear envelope, invading microtubules, and chromosomes being captured by the spindle—events shown in the prometaphase image.

prompt.text: Centrosomes are now at opposite ends of the cell.

questions.explanation: By metaphase, centrosomes have completed migration to opposite poles. A bipolar spindle is fully formed and ready to separate sister chromatids.

prompt.text: The chromosomes now arrive at the "metaphase plate", a plate equidistant from the centrosome spindle poles.

questions.explanation: Metaphase is defined by chromosomes aligned at the metaphase plate, equidistant from both spindle poles. This alignment ensures equal distribution of chromatids to daughter cells.
The metaphase image shows chromosomes congressed at the cell equator with spindle fibers attached. This is the staging point immediately before sister chromatid separation.

prompt.text: Shortest stage of mitosis.

questions.explanation: Anaphase is the shortest mitotic stage because cohesin cleavage triggers rapid, simultaneous separation of all sister chromatids. Chromosomes move quickly toward opposite poles.

prompt.text: Cohesion proteins are cleaved.

questions.explanation: Cohesin proteins holding sister chromatids together are cleaved at the onset of anaphase. Their removal allows sisters to separate and move toward opposite poles.

prompt.text: Sister chromatids separate abruptly.

questions.explanation: Sister chromatids separate abruptly in anaphase once centromeric cohesin is destroyed. Each former chromatid is then pulled poleward as an independent chromosome.

prompt.text: Sister chromatids become independent chromosomes.

questions.explanation: When sisters separate in anaphase, each chromatid becomes a full chromosome in its own right. The cell briefly doubles its chromosome count until cytokinesis partitions them.

prompt.text: Daughter chromosomes begin heading to opposite sides as kinetochore microtubules shorten.

questions.explanation: In anaphase, kinetochore microtubules shorten, pulling daughter chromosomes toward opposite poles. Motor proteins and microtubule depolymerization drive this movement.
The anaphase image shows sister chromatids moving toward opposite spindle poles. This is the key moment when identical genetic material is partitioned between future nuclei.

prompt.text: Two daughter nuclei form.

questions.explanation: Telophase reverses prophase: two distinct daughter nuclei form at opposite poles. Nuclear division is essentially complete before cytokinesis splits the cytoplasm.

prompt.text: Nuclear envelopes form from the fragments of parent cell's old nuclear envelope and portions of the endomembrane.

questions.explanation: During telophase, nuclear envelope vesicles fuse around each chromosome set, re-forming intact nuclei from ER and parent envelope fragments.

prompt.text: Nucleoli reappear.

questions.explanation: Nucleoli reappear in telophase as ribosomal RNA transcription resumes in the newly forming nuclei. Their return signals restoration of interphase nuclear organization.

prompt.text: Chromosomes become less condensed.

questions.explanation: Chromosomes decondense in telophase back into chromatin, making them less visible under the microscope. Decondensation accompanies the return to interphase gene expression.

prompt.text: Remaining spindle microtubules are depolymerized.

questions.explanation: Remaining spindle microtubules depolymerize in telophase as the mitotic apparatus disassembles. The cell prepares to return to interphase once division finishes.
Telophase shows decondensing chromosomes enclosed in re-forming nuclei at opposite poles—the final stage of nuclear division before cytokinesis.

prompt.text: Sister chromatids are attached together but a structure known as a __.

questions.explanation: Sister chromatids are held together at the centromere, the constricted region where kinetochores attach and cohesin links the two copies. Centrosomes organize spindle poles; kinetochores bind microtubules.

prompt.text: All body cells, not including sex cells, are known as __.

questions.explanation: Somatic cells are all body cells except gametes—they are typically diploid and undergo mitosis for growth and repair. Gametes and germ cells are specialized for reproduction.

prompt.text: Sister chromatids are attached down their length by protein complexes called __.

questions.explanation: Cohesin protein complexes bind sister chromatids along their length after DNA replication, keeping them paired until anaphase. Condensins compact chromosomes; histones package DNA; centromeres are attachment sites.

prompt.text: A portion of chromatid to either side of the centromere is known as a(n) __.

questions.explanation: The segment of a chromatid on either side of the centromere is called an arm (short p arm, long q arm). Cohesins hold sisters together but are not the name for these chromosomal regions.

prompt.text: A portion of chromatid to either side of the centromere is known as a(n) __.

questions.explanation: The segment of a chromatid on either side of the centromere is called an arm (short p arm, long q arm). Cohesins hold sisters together but are not the name for these chromosomal regions.

prompt.text: T/F: Chromosomes, regardless of replication status, always have a centromere.

questions.explanation: Every chromosome has a centromere—the region where kinetochores assemble and sister chromatids attach—even before replication produces two chromatids. It defines the chromosome's attachment point to the spindle.

prompt.text: Which of the following best describes the distinction between mitosis and cytokinesis?

options: Mitosis is the division of the cytoplasm, while cytokinesis is the division of the nucleus.
Mitosis is the division of the nucleus, while cytokinesis is the division of the cytoplasm.
Mitosis occurs only in plant cells, while cytokinesis occurs only in animal cells.
Mitosis and cytokinesis are different names for the exact same process.
questions.answer: Mitosis is the division of the nucleus, while cytokinesis is the division of the cytoplasm.
questions.explanation: Mitosis divides the nucleus (karyokinesis), while cytokinesis divides the cytoplasm into two cells. They are sequential but distinct processes that together complete cell division.

prompt.text: T/F: Mitosis in plant cells requires centrioles.

questions.explanation: Plant cells form spindles without centrioles, using other microtubule-organizing centers. Centrioles are not required for mitosis in plants, unlike the common textbook emphasis on animal cells.

prompt.text: T/F: Plant cells do not form a clevage furrow during mitosis.

questions.explanation: Plant cells divide by forming a cell plate instead of a cleavage furrow during cytokinesis. The cleavage furrow is characteristic of animal cell cytokinesis, not plant cells.

prompt.text: T/F: Both prokaryotes and eukaryotes replicate via mitosis.

questions.explanation: Prokaryotes divide by binary fission, not mitosis—there is no nuclear envelope or mitotic spindle. Mitosis is a eukaryotic process for partitioning a nucleus between daughter cells.

prompt.text: The process by which prokaryotic cells, such as bacteria, undergo cell division is known as __.

questions.explanation: Binary fission is the prokaryotic mode of reproduction: the circular chromosome replicates and the cell splits in two. Mitosis, budding, and fragmentation are eukaryotic or other mechanisms.

prompt.text: The activity of Maturation-Promoting Factor (MPF) peaks at the end of G2 to trigger the onset of mitosis. This peak is directly caused by the accumulation of which molecules?

options: Cyclins that bind with Cdk to form active complexes
Growth factors that initiate DNA replication
APC/C complexes that degrade sister chromatids
questions.answer: Cyclins that bind with Cdk to form active complexes
questions.explanation: MPF activity peaks when cyclin accumulates and binds Cdk to form an active kinase complex at the G2/M transition. Cyclin alone is inactive; APC/C degrades cyclin later to turn MPF off.

prompt.text: Most human cells are in the __ phase.

questions.explanation: Most human cells spend the longest part of the cell cycle in G1 (or G0 if non-dividing), carrying out growth and normal functions. S, G2, and mitosis occupy much less total time.

prompt.text: MPF (Maturation-Promoting Factor) initiates its own inactivation during anaphase by triggering a process that leads to the __.

options: Export of the complex out of the nucleus
questions.explanation: MPF inactivates in anaphase when APC/C ubiquitinates and degrades its cyclin subunit, dropping kinase activity. Without cyclin, the Cdk subunit cannot drive further mitotic events.

prompt.text: Chromosomes are divided into two sections by the centromere; the shorter arm is designated as the __ arm, while the longer arm is the __ arm.

questions.explanation: Cytogenetic convention labels the short chromosomal arm p and the long arm q. These arm designations describe regions flanking the centromere on each chromatid.

prompt.text: In a human somatic cell that typically contains 46 chromosomes, how many sister chromatids are present immediately following the completion of S phase?

questions.explanation: After S phase, each of the 46 chromosomes consists of two sister chromatids, giving 92 chromatids total. Chromatid count doubles during replication even though chromosome number stays 46 until anaphase.
